abstract: Developing efficient solar-to-hydrogen conversion technologies depends heavily on the discovery of high-performance catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). In this study, we investigate the mechanisms behind the exceptional performance of mixed nickel-iron oxyhydroxides (Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH), which exhibit a 500-fold increase in OER activity compared to their pure nickel and iron counterparts—positioning them among the most active OER catalysts known in alkaline media. Using operando high-energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD) X-ray absorption spectroscopy, we show that Fe³⁺ ions in the mixed lattice occupy octahedral sites characterized by unusually short Fe–O bond distances. This unique coordination environment is driven by edge-sharing with the surrounding [NiO₆] framework. Through density functional theory calculations, we demonstrate that this structural motif optimizes the adsorption energies of OER intermediates at the iron sites, significantly lowering the overpotential. Conversely, our findings indicate that the nickel sites remain spectator species and do not serve as active centers for water oxidation.
